Moudon

VDVaud

Create Account (Free) Or try a free trial story first

Story Ideas

Each story is rooted in real local history and landmarks

The chocolate secret of the Broye

Young Daniel Peter can't get his milk-and-chocolate mixture to work, no matter how hard he tries. A magical journey down the Broye river to Vevey leads him to his neighbour Henri Nestlé, where he discovers the secret of condensed milk. Together they create something the world has never tasted before — milk chocolate.

Create This Story

Daniel Peter was born in Moudon on 9 March 1836. In 1875 he invented milk chocolate by combining chocolate with condensed milk developed by his neighbour Henri Nestlé in Vevey. The brand Gala Peter launched in 1887, and in 1879 Peter and Nestlé formed a partnership that grew into the Nestlé company. Moudon lies at the confluence of the Broye and Mérine rivers in the heart of the Broye valley.

The two towns on the hill

A child living in the Ville-Basse discovers a hidden stairway that leads to the Ville-Haute after dark. Each medieval house on the Rue du Château reveals a different century of Moudon's past, from Roman legionnaires to Savoyard knights. By morning, the child has walked through two thousand years of history without ever leaving the old town.

Create This Story

Moudon's Ville-Haute occupies a rocky ridge between the Broye and Mérine rivers, with houses rising as extensions of the sandstone cliffs. The Rue du Château is the oldest street, lined with arcaded houses. The Ville-Basse developed during the Savoyard period around two hospices. Originally the Celtic settlement of Minnodunum, then a Roman vicus on the road from Vevey to Avenches, Moudon has been continuously inhabited for over two thousand years.

The belfry clock strikes thirteen

When the Château de Rochefort's belfry chimes an impossible thirteenth stroke at midnight, a child is transported to the year 1260. Peter of Savoy has arrived to make Moudon the capital of Vaud, and the town buzzes with builders, merchants, and knights. The child must find a way back before the clock strikes thirteen again.

Create This Story

The Château de Rochefort stands at the highest point of the Ville-Haute. Originally 14th-century merchants' houses, it was transformed into a seigneurial residence in 1595. Around 1730 a municipal belfry was added. Peter II of Savoy, known as 'Le Petit Charlemagne', established Moudon as the seat of the bailliage de Vaud around 1260, making it the administrative and judicial capital. The town received franchises in 1285.

The painter and the firebrands

During Les Brandons, Moudon's traditional carnival, a child follows a mysterious masked figure through the celebration. Each float comes alive with scenes painted by Eugène Burnand, and the Bonhomme Hiver must be convinced that spring is truly coming. Only by understanding the ancient meaning of the firebrands can the child help winter let go.

Create This Story

Les Brandons de Moudon is one of the major carnivals of western Switzerland, held on the first weekend of Lent. The name comes from the German word Brand (ember). A Bernese ordinance of 1597 mentions March fires in the pagan manner. The carnival nearly died out in the 1960s but was revived in the 1980s, with the pivotal 1982 edition introducing Guggenmusiks. Eugène Burnand (1850-1921) was born at the Château de Billens in Moudon; his best-known work hangs in the Musée d'Orsay in Paris.

Gustave Roud's lost photograph

A child finds a forgotten photograph by Gustave Roud showing a landscape of the Haut-Jorat that no longer exists. Following the poet's footsteps through meadows and farms near Carrouge, the child discovers what the land remembers. Each field and tree whispers a poem, and by sunset the lost landscape reappears for one fleeting moment.

Create This Story

Gustave Roud (1897-1976) is the most beloved poet of Suisse romande and co-founder of modern French-language Swiss literature with Charles-Ferdinand Ramuz. He lived from age 11 at a farm in Carrouge near Moudon until his death. His poetry celebrates the landscapes of the Haut-Jorat. He was also a prolific photographer with some 13,000 images. His most famous work is Air de la solitude.

The royal chef's first recipe

Young Gabriel Tschumi, about to leave Moudon for England, must prepare one perfect dish from his mother's kitchen to prove he is ready to cook for a queen. Ingredients come from Moudon's market stalls, and each vendor tells a story about the town. If the dish is good enough, a new life in the royal palace awaits.

Create This Story

Gabriel Tschumi (1882-1957) was born in Moudon and emigrated to Britain in 1899 at age 16. He became Master Chef to three British monarchs — Queen Victoria, Edward VII, and George V. His memoirs, Royal Chef (1954), recount decades of life in the royal kitchens.

The pilgrims' fountain

A child sitting by the Fontaine de Moïse meets a medieval pilgrim walking the Via Jacobi to Santiago de Compostela. Together they explore the Église Saint-Étienne, where the ancient organ plays a melody that guides lost travellers home. The fountain's water, the pilgrim says, has been blessing journeys since 1559.

Create This Story

The Fontaine de Moïse has an iron basin ring from 1557, with the column and statue of Moses sculpted by Laurent Perroud in 1559. The Église Saint-Étienne is the largest parish church in the canton of Vaud, known locally as the Cathédrale de la Broye. It houses the oldest functioning organ in the canton, dating from 1764. The church is a stop on the Via Jacobi, the Swiss pilgrimage route to Santiago de Compostela.

The Estates assemble

In the Maison des États, a child accidentally sits in the seat reserved for the representative of the Pays de Vaud and is transported to a 15th-century assembly. The child must speak for the townspeople and convince the Savoyard lords to keep Moudon's precious franchises. The fate of the town rests on a single speech.

Create This Story

The Maison des États du Pays de Vaud is a late-Gothic building on the Rue du Château, built around 1500, listed as a Swiss heritage site of national significance. As capital of the bailliage de Vaud, Moudon served as the meeting place for the États de Vaud — the assembly of nobles, clergy, and town representatives. Their tradition helped shape civic consciousness that contributed to the creation of the canton of Vaud in 1803.
